sections.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
sections.title: RBI Monetary Policy: Quantitative and Qualitative Tools
sections.focus: Understanding the instruments used by the RBI to control money supply and direct credit flow.
sections.rationale: This is a foundational economic concept covering the core tools of monetary policy, which is highly examinable in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: QUANTITATIVE TOOLS (Volume of Credit) - Directly impact the overall money supply: REPO RATE (Lending Rate): Rate at which RBI lends short-term to banks. Main signal rate. REVERSE REPO RATE (Borrowing Rate): Rate at which RBI borrows from banks. CRR (Cash Reserve Ratio): % of net demand & time liabilities (NDTL) banks must keep as CASH with RBI. No interest paid. SLR (Statutory Liquidity Ratio): % of NDTL banks must maintain in LIQUID ASSETS. QUALITATIVE TOOLS (Direction of Credit) - Selectively influence credit flow to specific sectors: MORAL SUASION... MARGIN REQUIREMENTS... DIRECT ACTION...
evidenceBullets: Repo Rate is the rate at which RBI lends short-term to banks; increasing it decreases money supply.
CRR is the percentage of NDTL banks must keep as cash with RBI, earning no interest.
SLR is the percentage of NDTL banks must maintain in liquid assets like gold, bonds, or cash.
Qualitative tools include Moral Suasion, Margin Requirements (changing loan-to-value ratio), and Direct Action.

sections.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
sections.title: RBI Surplus Transfer and Economic Capital Framework (ECF)
sections.focus: The mechanism, framework, and components involved in the transfer of RBI's surplus to the Central Government.
sections.rationale: The ECF and surplus transfer are critical aspects of RBI-Government relations and fiscal management, frequently tested in the exam.
sections.sectionExcerpt: RBI has transferred Rs 2.69 Lakh Crore surplus to centre. The transferable surplus for 2024-25 has been arrived at on the basis of the revised Economic Capital Framework (ECF)... Genesis: ECF was adopted by RBI in 2019, based on the recommendations of the Bimal Jalan Committee. Components of Economic Capital under ECF: Realized Equity... Contingent Risk Buffer (CRB): Component of RBI's realized equity to provide for monetary and financial stability... Revaluation Balances...
evidenceBullets: RBI transferred Rs 2.69 Lakh Crore surplus to the centre for 2024-25 based on the Economic Capital Framework (ECF).
The ECF was adopted in 2019 based on the recommendations of the Bimal Jalan Committee.
RBI's income sources include interest on government securities, liquidity management tools, seigniorage, and foreign currency investments.
Economic Capital components under ECF include Realized Equity, Contingent Risk Buffer (CRB), and Revaluation Balances.

sections.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
sections.title: India's Forex Reserves and Gold Holdings
sections.focus: Composition of India's foreign exchange reserves and the rationale and risks associated with increasing gold reserves.
sections.rationale: Forex reserves composition is a classic UPSC topic, and the recent surge in gold reserves adds current relevance.
sections.sectionExcerpt: According to the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) latest foreign exchange reserves data, gold reserves have reached to $102.3 billion. This has taken the share of the gold in the foreign reserves to nearly 15%... Why is RBI increasing gold reserves? Diversification... Risk Mitigation... Hedge against Inflation... Risk Associated with Storing Gold: Reduced Liquidity and Flexibility... Zero Yield... Components of Foreign Exchange Reserves: Foreign Currency Assets (FCA)... Gold Reserves... Holdings with IMF... Special Drawing Rights... Reserve Tranche Position (RTP)...
evidenceBullets: Gold reserves constitute nearly 15% of India's foreign reserves, reaching $102.3 billion.
RBI increases gold reserves for diversification (de-dollarization), risk mitigation, and hedging against inflation.
Risks of storing gold include reduced liquidity, zero yield, and high storage and security costs.
Forex reserves consist of Foreign Currency Assets (FCA), Gold, Special Drawing Rights (SDR), and Reserve Tranche Position (RTP).

sections.subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
sections.title: Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021
sections.focus: Expansion of the RB-IOS to include Co-operative Banks and its core objectives.
sections.rationale: Regulatory changes and grievance redressal mechanisms in banking are important for polity and economy sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Central and State Co-operative Banks have been brought under the ambit of Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 under Banking regulation act, 1949. About Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 (RB-IOS, 2021) - Objective: Provide customers of regulated entities (REs) a speedy, cost-effective and expeditious alternate grievance redress mechanism.
evidenceBullets: Central and State Co-operative Banks are now covered under the Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021.
The inclusion of Co-operative Banks was done under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
The scheme aims to provide a speedy and cost-effective alternate grievance redress mechanism for customers of regulated entities.

sections.title: RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme
sections.focus: Coverage criteria, integrated schemes, and compensation powers of the RBI Ombudsman.
sections.rationale: Highly testable due to specific coverage thresholds and compensation limits.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme covers Commercial Banks, UCBs with deposits of 50 crore, NBFCs with assets of 100 crore, System Participants, and Credit Information Companies. It integrates the Banking, NBFC, and Digital Transactions Ombudsman schemes under a One Nation One Ombudsman approach. The Ombudsman can award up to 20 lakh in compensation and 1 lakh for distress.
evidenceBullets: Covers UCBs with deposits of 50 crore and NBFCs with assets of 100 crore.
Integrates Banking (2006), NBFC (2018), and Digital Transactions (2019) Ombudsman schemes.
Ombudsman can award up to 20 lakh in compensation and 1 lakh for mental distress.

sections.title: RBI Liquidity and Debt Management Tools
sections.focus: Mechanisms of Standing Deposit Facility (SDF), Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR), and Switch Auctions.
sections.rationale: Core monetary policy tools; SDF and VRRR are critical for liquidity management and frequently tested in UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Standing Deposit Facility (SDF) absorbs liquidity without collateral, replacing the Fixed Rate Reverse Repo as the LAF corridor floor. It is available every day. Variable Rate Reverse Repo (VRRR) absorbs excess money for short periods at market-decided rates. Switch Auctions manage public debt maturity by replacing short-term securities with longer-dated ones via the e-Kuber portal.
evidenceBullets: SDF absorbs liquidity from commercial banks without collateral and replaced the Fixed Rate Reverse Repo as the LAF floor.
SDF is available every day, including holidays and weekends.
VRRR interest rates are decided by the market, unlike the fixed Reverse Repo Rate.
Switch Auctions replace short-term government securities with longer-dated ones to manage debt maturity.

sections.title: Co-lending Norms and Payment Aggregators
sections.focus: Regulatory guidelines for Co-Lending Arrangements (CLA) and Payment Aggregators.
sections.rationale: Important regulatory updates for NBFCs, banks, and fintech entities.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Under Co-Lending Arrangements between banks and NBFCs, each Regulated Entity must retain a minimum 10 percent share of the loans. Lenders can claim Priority Sector Lending status for their share. Uniform asset classification applies; if one lender tags a loan as NPA, others must follow. Payment Aggregators facilitate merchant payments, are incorporated under the Companies Act 2013, and non-bank PAs require RBI authorisation under the PSS Act 2007.
evidenceBullets: Co-lending norms require each Regulated Entity to retain a minimum 10 percent share of the loans.
Co-lenders must uniformly classify assets; an NPA tag by one requires the same by others.
Non-bank Payment Aggregators must obtain RBI authorisation under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.

sections.title: Ways and Means Advances (WMA)
sections.focus: Purpose, limits, interest rates, and duration of WMAs for central and state governments.
sections.rationale: WMA is a standard fiscal-monetary interface topic with clear factual parameters.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ways and Means Advances (WMA) are temporary advances by RBI to central and state governments to cover receipt-payment mismatches, not to finance fiscal deficits. Authorized by the RBI Act 1934, the interest rate is the repo rate, while overdrafts are charged 2 percent above the repo rate. WMAs must be fully paid off within 3 months.
evidenceBullets: WMA is a temporary advance for receipt-payment mismatches and does not finance the fiscal deficit.
Available to both the Central government and state governments/Union Territories.
Interest rate on WMA is the RBI repo rate, and overdrafts are 2 percent above the repo rate.
WMA must be fully paid off within 3 months.

sections.title: RBI Digital Initiatives and Certificate of Deposits
sections.focus: Unified Lending Interface, CBDC sandbox, and characteristics of Certificate of Deposits.
sections.rationale: Emerging digital infrastructure and traditional money market instruments (CDs) are prime targets for prelims questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: RBI initiatives include the Unified Lending Interface (ULI), a Digital Public Infrastructure for credit delivery, and a CBDC retail sandbox for fintechs. Tokenisation of Certificate of Deposits (CDs) is also introduced. CDs are negotiable money market instruments issued at a discount to face value by Scheduled Commercial Banks (excluding RRBs and LABs) and select FIs, with a maturity of 7 days to one year.
evidenceBullets: Unified Lending Interface (ULI) is a Digital Public Infrastructure aiming to enhance credit delivery.
Certificate of Deposits (CDs) are issued by Scheduled Commercial Banks, excluding Regional Rural Banks and Local Area Banks.
CDs are issued at a discount on face value with a maturity period ranging from 7 days to one year.

sections.title: Self-Regulatory Organisation for NBFCs
sections.focus: Recognition of FIDC and eligibility criteria for SROs under RBI framework.
sections.rationale: Small but specific regulatory development regarding NBFC oversight.
sections.sectionExcerpt: RBI granted Self-Regulatory Organisation (SRO) status to the Finance Industry Development Council (FIDC) to oversee NBFCs. Backed by RBI's Omnibus Framework 2024, an eligible SRO must be set up as a not-for-profit company registered under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013.
evidenceBullets: RBI granted SRO status to the Finance Industry Development Council (FIDC) to oversee NBFCs.
An SRO must be established as a not-for-profit company under Section 8 of the Companies Act, 2013.

sections.title: Self-Regulatory Organizations (SROs)
sections.focus: Regulatory guidelines, responsibilities, and governance structure of SROs.
sections.rationale: SRO governance and responsibilities are highly testable as statement-based questions in UPSC prelims, particularly the specific numerical criteria for shareholding and independent directors.
sections.sectionExcerpt: SROs must have adequate net worth and diversified shareholding, with no entity holding 10% or more of its paid-up share capital. They are responsible for framing codes of conduct, dispute resolution, ensuring regulatory compliance, and detecting early warning signals. Governance requires at least one-third of the Board of Directors, including the Chairperson, to be independent.
evidenceBullets: No entity shall hold 10% or more of an SRO's paid-up share capital.
Responsibilities include framing a code of conduct, dispute resolution, and ensuring regulatory compliance.
At least one-third of Board members, including the Chairperson, must be independent.

sections.title: Non-Banking Financial Company (NBFC)
sections.focus: Core characteristics and regulatory distinctions of NBFCs compared to commercial banks.
sections.rationale: The distinction between NBFCs and banks is a classic UPSC concept, particularly regarding demand deposits and payment systems.
sections.sectionExcerpt: NBFCs are registered under the Companies Act, 1956, engaging in lending and investment while excluding agriculture, industry, and real estate activities. Unlike banks, NBFCs cannot accept demand deposits, are outside the payment and settlement system, and cannot issue cheques drawn on themselves.
evidenceBullets: Registered under the Companies Act, 1956.
Cannot accept demand deposits.
Outside the payment and settlement system and cannot issue cheques on themselves.

sections.title: Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) and Amalgamation
sections.focus: Amalgamation phases, ownership structure, regulatory oversight, and statutory requirements of RRBs.
sections.rationale: RRBs have a unique ownership structure and specific PSL/CRAR targets that are frequently tested in UPSC. The recent amalgamation news makes it highly relevant.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Central Government notified the amalgamation of 26 RRBs under the 'One State One RRB' principle, reducing the total to 28 RRBs. Established in 1975 on the Narasimham Working Group's recommendation, RRBs are jointly owned by the GoI (50%), State Government (15%), and a sponsoring commercial bank (35%). They are regulated by RBI, supervised by NABARD, and treated as cooperative societies for tax purposes. RRBs must allocate 75% of ANBC to Priority Sector Lending (PSL) and maintain a 9% CRAR.
evidenceBullets: Amalgamation reduces RRBs to 28 across 26 states and 2 UTs.
Established in 1975 based on Narasimham Working Group recommendations.
Ownership: GoI (50%), State Government (15%), Sponsoring Bank (35%).
Regulated by RBI and supervised by NABARD.
Treated as cooperative societies under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Must allocate 75% of ANBC to Priority Sector Lending (PSL).
Must maintain a Capital to Risk-Weighted Assets Ratio (CRAR) of 9%.

sections.subtopic: Small Finance Banks and PSL Norms
sections.title: Small Finance Banks (SFBs) and Universal Bank Transition
sections.focus: Eligibility criteria for SFBs to transition to Universal Banks and foundational facts about SFBs.
sections.rationale: Covers the specific RBI criteria for SFB transition and the basic regulatory framework of SFBs, which are highly testable for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The RBI granted 'in-principle' approval to AU Small Finance Bank to transition to a universal bank. Eligibility criteria include scheduled status for 5 years, stock listing, minimum net worth of ₹1,000 crore, net profits in the last two FYs, and gross/net NPAs ≤3% and ≤1% respectively. SFBs were announced in the 2014-15 budget, registered under the Companies Act 2013, and licensed under Section 22 of the Banking Regulation Act 1949. Minimum paid-up equity capital is ₹200 crore (₹100 crore initially for UCBs transitioning to SFBs).
evidenceBullets: Transition requires 5 years of scheduled status, ₹1,000 crore minimum net worth, and stock exchange listing.
Asset quality for transition requires Gross NPA ≤ 3% and Net NPA ≤ 1% over the last two FYs.
SFBs are registered under the Companies Act, 2013 and licensed under Section 22 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
Minimum paid-up voting equity capital for SFBs is ₹200 crores.

sections.subtopic: Small Finance Banks and PSL Norms
sections.title: Priority Sector Lending (PSL) Norms for SFBs
sections.focus: Changes in PSL targets for SFBs and the general framework of Priority Sector Lending.
sections.rationale: PSL targets are frequently asked in UPSC. The reduction in the PSL target for SFBs from 75% to 60% is a significant policy shift.
sections.sectionExcerpt: RBI issued new PSL rules for SFBs under Section 22(1) of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949. Effective FY 2025-26, the total PSL requirement for SFBs is reduced from 75% to 60% of Adjusted Net Bank Credit (ANBC). The 40% mandatory allocation remains, but the flexible allocation is reduced to 20% (usable for non-PSL secured loans). PSL was established in the 1970s and applies to Commercial Banks, RRBs, SFBs, LABs, and UCBs. Categories include Agriculture, MSME, Export Credit, Education, Housing, Social Infrastructure, and Renewable Energy.
evidenceBullets: New PSL target for SFBs is reduced to 60% of ANBC (effective FY 2025-26), down from the earlier 75%.
The 60% target comprises 40% mandatory allocation to PSL sectors and 20% flexible allocation.
PSL framework applies to Commercial Banks, RRBs, SFBs, Local Area Banks, and Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks.
PSL categories include Agriculture, MSME, Export Credit, Education, Housing, Social Infrastructure, and Renewable Energy.

sections.subtopic: Small Finance Banks and PSL Norms
sections.title: SARFAESI Act, 2002 and NPA Recovery
sections.focus: Anomalies in the SARFAESI Act regarding borrower redemption rights and the mechanisms for NPA recovery.
sections.rationale: The Supreme Court's observation on the contradiction between Section 13(8) and Rules 8 & 9 makes this a prime candidate for legal/economic questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Supreme Court highlighted a contradiction in the SARFAESI Act, 2002: Section 13(8) states borrowers lose the right to redeem a secured asset once an auction notice is published, whereas Rules 8 & 9 allow redemption up until the auction date. The Act aims to recover NPAs (loans overdue for >90 days) for financial institutions. Key features include a mandatory 60-day demand notice to borrowers and recovery methods like securitisation and asset reconstruction via RBI-regulated Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARCs).
evidenceBullets: Contradiction exists between SARFAESI Act Section 13(8) (borrower loses redemption right upon auction notice publication) and Rules 8 & 9 (redemption allowed until auction date).
A loan becomes an NPA when principal or interest remains overdue for more than 90 days.
The Act mandates a 60-day demand notice to borrowers before initiating asset recovery actions.
Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARCs) are registered and regulated by the RBI to buy NPAs and clean up bank balance sheets.

sections.title: Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARC) and SARFAESI Act
sections.focus: Mechanisms for NPA resolution, powers under SARFAESI, and borrower rights via DRT.
sections.rationale: SARFAESI and DRT are foundational concepts in banking regulation and NPA recovery, frequently tested in UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: ARC is a company incorporated under the Companies Act and registered with RBI under SARFAESI Act. Court-Free Enforcement: Secured creditors like banks and financial institutions, can take possession and sell assets except for agricultural land under Section 13 without judicial approval. Central Database: Established Central Registry for registration of transaction of securitisation and reconstruction of financial assets. Borrower Rights: Borrowers can approach the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) to rectify their grievances against the creditor or authorised officer. DRT are established under the Recovery of Debts and Bankruptcy Act, 1993.
evidenceBullets: ARCs are registered with RBI under the SARFAESI Act.
SARFAESI allows secured creditors to take possession and sell assets without judicial approval, except for agricultural land.
Borrowers can approach the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) for grievances.
DRTs are established under the Recovery of Debts and Bankruptcy Act, 1993.

sections.title: SWIFT Messaging Network and ISO 20022
sections.focus: Global financial messaging standards and alternatives like BRICS Pay.
sections.rationale: International payment systems and alternatives like BRICS Pay are highly relevant for UPSC Economy and International Relations.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Indian banks have accelerated adoption of the SWIFT messaging framework, ISO 20022. The ISO 20022 is an open global standard for financial information. About SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) It is a secure messaging network facilitating global financial transactions between over 11,000 institutions across 200 countries. Genesis: Founded in 1973 by 239 banks from 15 countries. Headquarters: Belgium. Note: BRICS Pay is a proposed cross-border payment system to facilitate trade and financial transactions among BRICS member to reduce dependence on SWIFT.
evidenceBullets: ISO 20022 is an open global standard for financial information.
SWIFT is a secure messaging network for global financial transactions, headquartered in Belgium.
BRICS Pay is a proposed cross-border payment system to reduce dependence on SWIFT.

sections.title: India Post Payments Bank (IPPB) and Payment Banks
sections.focus: Structure, functions, and regulatory limits of Payment Banks in India.
sections.rationale: Payment banks have specific regulatory constraints such as deposit limits and no lending, which are classic UPSC prelims traps.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Genesis: 2018 as a 100% government-owned payments bank under the Department of Posts, Ministry of Communications. Payment banks are specialized financial institutions designed to provide basic banking services to the underbanked and unbanked population. Aim: Leverages the extensive postal network to bring banking services to the doorstep of every citizen. Services offered: Saving/ Current account, Direct Benefits Transfers, Bill and Utility Payments, access to 3rd Party products such as loans, insurance, etc. It can accept deposits (up to 2 lakh), and enable money transfers and bill payments, but cannot give loans or issue credit cards.
evidenceBullets: IPPB is a 100% government-owned payments bank under the Department of Posts.
Payment banks can accept deposits up to 2 lakh.
Payment banks cannot give loans or issue credit cards.
They offer services like Direct Benefits Transfers, bill payments, and access to 3rd party products like insurance.

sections.title: Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) Framework and Capital Adequacy Ratio
sections.focus: RBI's supervisory action framework for banks and NBFCs, and the definition of CAR.
sections.rationale: PCA and CAR are critical banking regulation tools used by RBI to maintain financial stability.
sections.sectionExcerpt: About 500 Primary Urban Co-operative Banks (UCBs) under the RBI's supervisory action framework will shift to the Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) framework from April 1, 2025. RBI had introduced a PCA Framework for Scheduled Commercial Banks in 2002 & for UCB in July, 2024. PCA framework for UCBs replaced the existing Supervisory Action Framework (SAF). Applicability: It applies to both banks and NBFCs. Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), Asset Quality and Profitability are the key areas for monitoring. CAR is the ratio of total capital to total risk-weighted assets in percentage terms. It is also called Capital to Risk-weighted Asset Ratio (CRAR).
evidenceBullets: PCA framework applies to both banks and NBFCs.
For UCBs, the PCA framework replaced the Supervisory Action Framework (SAF).
Key monitoring areas under PCA include Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), Asset Quality, and Profitability.
CAR (or CRAR) is the ratio of total capital to total risk-weighted assets in percentage terms.

sections.title: Financial Fraud Risk Indicator (FRI)
sections.focus: Risk-based metric for mobile numbers associated with financial fraud.
sections.rationale: Emerging cybersecurity and financial fraud initiatives are relevant for both Economy and Internal Security.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Launched in May 2025 by DoT's Digital Intelligence Unit (DIU). A risk-based metric that classifies a mobile number to have been associated with Medium, High, or Very High risk of financial fraud. This classification is an outcome of inputs obtained from various stakeholders including reporting on Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C's) National Cybercrime Reporting Portal (NCRP), DoT's Chakshu platform, and Intelligence shared by banks and financial institutions.
evidenceBullets: FRI is launched by the Department of Telecommunications' (DoT) Digital Intelligence Unit (DIU).
It classifies mobile numbers into Medium, High, or Very High risk of financial fraud.
Inputs are gathered from I4C's NCRP, DoT's Chakshu platform, and banks.

sections.title: Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management (FRBM) Act
sections.focus: Provisions, targets, and recent CAG compliance findings regarding the FRBM Act.
sections.rationale: The FRBM Act is a core macroeconomic topic for UPSC, with specific targets and recent CAG findings providing excellent material for statement-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The FRBM Act, 2003 aims to foster inter-generational equity and macroeconomic stability. It mandates the CAG to conduct an annual compliance review and requires three statements to be laid before Parliament: Macro-economic Framework, Medium Term Fiscal Policy, and Fiscal Policy Strategy. Key targets include a fiscal deficit of 3% of GDP, general government debt limit of 60% (Central 40%), and additional guarantees capped at 0.5% of GDP. The 2018 amendment removed Revenue and Effective Revenue Deficit targets. The recent CAG report noted central government debt peaked at 61.38% in FY21, while unrealized tax revenues surged to 21.30 trillion by FY23.
evidenceBullets: FRBM Act mandates CAG to conduct an annual compliance review.
Requires Macro-economic Framework, Medium Term Fiscal Policy, and Fiscal Policy Strategy statements with the Budget.
Original fiscal deficit target is 3% of GDP; revised path targets under 4.5%.
Debt limits set at 60% for General Government and 40% for Central Government.
Targets for Revenue and Effective Revenue Deficit were removed by the 2018 Amendment.
CAG reported central government debt-to-GDP peaked at 61.38% in FY 2020-21.

sections.title: Key Fiscal Terms and Deficits
sections.focus: Definitions and implications of various government deficits and fiscal slippage.
sections.rationale: Conceptual clarity on deficits is frequently tested in UPSC Prelims, especially the distinctions between fiscal, primary, revenue, and effective revenue deficits.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Fiscal Deficit is the difference between total expenditure and total receipts excluding borrowing. Primary Deficit is Fiscal Deficit minus interest payments. Revenue Deficit is the excess of revenue expenditure over revenue receipts, representing net dissaving. Effective Revenue Deficit is Revenue Deficit minus grants for the creation of capital assets. Fiscal Slippage occurs when actual fiscal performance deviates from targets, often due to farm loan waivers or tax cuts.
evidenceBullets: Fiscal Deficit excludes borrowing from total receipts.
Primary Deficit is calculated by subtracting interest payments from Fiscal Deficit.
Revenue Deficit represents net dissaving for present consumption without capital formation.
Effective Revenue Deficit excludes grants for creation of Capital Assets from Revenue Deficit.
Fiscal Slippage can be caused by farm loan waivers or reductions in tax collections like GST and VAT.

sections.title: Indian Tax Structure: Direct vs. Indirect Taxes
sections.focus: Characteristics, administration, and examples of direct and indirect taxes in India.
sections.rationale: The distinction between progressive direct taxes and regressive indirect taxes, along with their respective administrative bodies, forms the foundational basis of taxation questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Indian tax structure is divided into Direct and Indirect taxes. Direct taxes (e.g., Income, Corporate, Capital Gains) are progressive, administered by the CBDT, and their incidence and impact fall on the same person. Indirect taxes (e.g., GST, Customs, Excise) are regressive, administered by the CBIC, and their burden is shifted to the consumer. Direct taxes are harder to collect due to evasion risks, whereas indirect taxes are embedded in prices.
evidenceBullets: Direct taxes are progressive in nature, meaning higher income attracts a higher tax rate.
The burden of direct taxes cannot be shifted, whereas the burden of indirect taxes is shifted to the buyer.
Direct taxes are administered by the CBDT, while indirect taxes are administered by the CBIC.
Indirect taxes are regressive, applying the same rate regardless of income.
Excise duty on petroleum and liquor remains outside the GST framework.

sections.title: Income Tax Act 2025
sections.focus: Key provisions of the new Income Tax Act 2025, including MAT, AMT, and the inclusion of virtual digital assets.
sections.rationale: This section covers a major legislative update. The distinctions between MAT and AMT, along with the expanded definition of undisclosed income to include virtual digital assets, are highly testable for UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The President has given her assent to the Income Tax Act 2025 which replaces the Income-Tax Act, 1961. The provisions of Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) and Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) have been separated into two sub-sections. MAT aims to bring into the tax net 'zero tax companies'... The provisions of MAT are applicable to a corporate taxpayer only. AMT has similar provisions as MAT which is applicable to non-corporate tax payers. Undisclosed Income: The definition of undisclosed income... is expanded to include virtual digital assets.
evidenceBullets: The Income Tax Act 2025 replaces the Income-Tax Act, 1961.
Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) applies exclusively to corporate taxpayers (zero tax companies).
Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) applies to non-corporate taxpayers.
The definition of undisclosed income for search cases now includes virtual digital assets.
Income tax authorities can access virtual digital spaces (e.g., email servers, social media) during search and seizure proceedings.
A dispute resolution panel is available for eligible assessees, including those in transfer pricing cases, non-residents, and foreign companies.

sections.title: Safe Harbour Rules and Advance Pricing Agreements (APAs)
sections.focus: CBDT's expansion of safe harbour rules and the framework of APAs for transfer pricing.
sections.rationale: Transfer pricing, APAs, and Safe Harbour rules form a cohesive cluster of corporate taxation concepts frequently tested in economics and finance sections.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) notifies amendments in Income-Tax Rules, 1962 to expand the scope of safe harbour rules. Increasing threshold for availing safe harbour from Rs. 200 Crore to Rs. 300 Crore. Including Lithium-Ion Batteries for use in electric or hybrid electric vehicles in definition of core auto components... Advance Pricing Agreements (APAs)... aims to provide certainty to taxpayers in the area of transfer pricing by specifying pricing methods and determining the arm's length price of international transactions.
evidenceBullets: The threshold for availing safe harbour was increased from Rs. 200 Crore to Rs. 300 Crore.
Lithium-Ion Batteries for electric/hybrid vehicles are now included in the definition of core auto components for safe harbour.
Safe harbour means the tax authority accepts the transfer price declared by the taxpayer to be at arm's length.
An Advance Pricing Agreement (APA) determines the arm's length price of international transactions for a maximum term of 5 years with no minimum period.
Transfer Pricing refers to the price of goods and services exchanged between companies under common ownership or control.
The Arm's Length Principle states that prices in related-party transactions must match prices in comparable unrelated-party transactions.

sections.title: Securities Transaction Tax (STT)
sections.focus: Definition, applicability, and purpose of the Securities Transaction Tax.
sections.rationale: STT is a specific direct tax instrument with clear applicability rules (equity, derivatives, mutual funds) suitable for standalone objective questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Securities Transaction Tax (STT) - It is a direct tax levied on transactions of securities conducted through a recognized stock exchange in India. Taxable securities include equity, derivatives, and unit of equity-oriented mutual fund. It is also applied on the transaction of options and futures. Introduced under the Finance Act, 2004. Aim: to curb tax evasion in the securities market.
evidenceBullets: STT is a direct tax levied on securities transactions conducted through recognized stock exchanges in India.
Taxable securities under STT include equity, derivatives, equity-oriented mutual funds, options, and futures.
STT was introduced under the Finance Act, 2004 to curb tax evasion in the securities market.

sections.title: Goods and Services Tax (GST) Reform
sections.focus: Evolution from GST 1.0 to GST 2.0, including constitutional backing, tax structure, and destination-based nature.
sections.rationale: GST fundamentals and the transition to a two-slab structure (GST 2.0) are critical indirect tax topics for UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The 56th meeting of the GST Council has approved Next-Gen GST reform (GST 2.0) that includes rate rationalization with a simplified two-slab structure. Evaluation of GST 1.0 - About GST: It is a single indirect tax on the supply of goods and services across India. It was Introduced by the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2017. It subsumed 17 different taxes and 13 cesses into one unified tax... Destination-Based Tax: Revenue goes to the state/UT where the goods or services are finally consumed.
evidenceBullets: GST 2.0 aims for rate rationalization with a simplified two-slab structure.
GST is a single indirect tax introduced by the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2017.
GST subsumed 17 different taxes and 13 cesses, eliminating the cascading effect of taxes.
GST is a destination-based tax, meaning revenue goes to the state or UT where goods/services are finally consumed.
IGST is levied on inter-state transactions, collected by the Centre, and shared with the states.

sections.title: GST Rate Rationalization and Relief Measures
sections.focus: Changes to the GST slab system and specific rate reductions for essential sectors.
sections.rationale: The shift to a two-slab system and specific exemptions, particularly for health insurance and lifesaving drugs, are highly testable facts for UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Two-slab system of 5% and 18%, removing the earlier 12% and 28% rate. A 40% Special De-Merit Rate applies to 'sin' goods and select luxuries, replacing the compensation cess levy. The median tax rate effectively moves from 12% to 5%. Relief for Citizens and Key Sectors: Health (18% to Exempt/5%): GST is exempted on all individual life and health insurance policies and their reinsurance.
evidenceBullets: A new two-slab system of 5% and 18% replaces the earlier 12% and 28% rates.
A 40% Special De-Merit Rate applies to 'sin' goods, replacing the compensation cess.
GST is now exempted on all individual life and health insurance policies.
Cement, air conditioners, and all TV sets have dropped from the 28% to the 18% slab.

sections.title: GST Administrative and Export Reforms
sections.focus: Institutional and procedural changes including GSTAT, registration ease, and export benefits.
sections.rationale: Institutional bodies like GSTAT and technical definitions like 'place of supply' for intermediary services are standard UPSC targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Dispute Resolution: The Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) is set to be operational for appeals before the end of September 2025. Registration Ease: A simplified, optional GST registration scheme is recommended for small and low-risk businesses. Export Benefits: Recommended that the place of supply for 'intermediary services' will be determined as per the location of the recipient of such services.
evidenceBullets: The Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) will be operational by September 2025.
A simplified optional GST registration scheme for small businesses will be operational from November 2025.
The place of supply for intermediary services is now determined by the location of the recipient, aiding Indian exporters.

sections.title: GST Council
sections.focus: Constitutional provisions, composition, and voting mechanism of the GST Council.
sections.rationale: The GST Council's constitutional basis (Article 279A) and unique voting weightage are classic polity and economy crossover questions frequently asked in UPSC.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Constitutional Basis: Established under Article 279A, the apex decision-making body on GST matters. Composition: Chaired by the Union Finance Minister, with the Union MoS (Revenue/Finance) and Finance Ministers of all states/UTs with legislatures as members. Voting: Uses a weighted system; Centre has one-third vote, states have two-thirds; decisions need three-fourths majority of weighted votes.
evidenceBullets: The GST Council is a constitutional body established under Article 279A.
It is chaired by the Union Finance Minister.
The Centre holds one-third of the voting weight, while states hold two-thirds.
Decisions in the GST Council require a three-fourths majority of weighted votes.

sections.title: Anti-Dumping Duty
sections.focus: Definition, mechanism of dumping, and GATT provisions.
sections.rationale: A fundamental trade remedy concept frequently tested in UPSC for its definition and WTO/GATT legality.
sections.sectionExcerpt: An anti-dumping duty is a protectionist tariff imposed on foreign imports dumped in the domestic market. Dumping occurs when a company exports a product at a significantly lower price than its domestic market price. The General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) permits countries to take action against dumping.
evidenceBullets: Anti-dumping duty is a protectionist tariff against dumped foreign imports.
Dumping means exporting a product at a price significantly lower than its domestic market price.
GATT allows countries to take action against dumping.

sections.title: Safeguard Duty
sections.focus: WTO provisions, purpose, application rules, and duration limits of safeguard duties.
sections.rationale: Safeguard duties have specific technical criteria (non-targeted principle, duration limits) that are highly examinable in UPSC prelims.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Safeguard Duty is a WTO measure imposed to protect domestic players from a sudden and significant increase in imports. In principle, it cannot be targeted at a particular country, though individual country quotas apply under exceptional disproportionate import increases. It generally lasts up to four years, extendable to eight years.
evidenceBullets: Safeguard Duty is a measure available to WTO Member countries.
Imposed to provide a level-playing field against sudden and significant import increases.
In principle, safeguard measures cannot be targeted at a particular country.
Individual country quotas can be applied in exceptional circumstances of disproportionate import increases.
Duration is typically up to four years, extendable to a maximum of eight years.

sections.title: Off-budget Borrowing
sections.focus: Definition, mechanisms via PSUs, purpose, and macroeconomic concerns.
sections.rationale: Off-budget borrowing is a critical fiscal policy concept related to transparency and fiscal deficit calculation, making it a prime target for economy questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Off-budget borrowing refers to government borrowing not explicitly included in the official budget. It is typically raised through government-controlled PSUs or entities dependent on budgetary support. Its purpose is to keep borrowings off the fiscal deficit books, creating an impression of prudence, but it reduces transparency and conceals the true debt burden.
evidenceBullets: Off-budget borrowing is not explicitly included in the official budget or approved by the legislature.
Raised through government-controlled PSUs or entities dependent on budgetary support.
Keeps borrowings off the fiscal deficit books to create an impression of fiscal prudence.
Concerns include reduced transparency, lack of accountability, and concealment of the true debt burden.

sections.title: Indian Financial Market Structure: Money vs. Capital Market
sections.focus: Distinctions between Money Market and Capital Market, their regulators, and key instruments.
sections.rationale: Fundamental economic concepts frequently tested in UPSC prelims, specifically the differences in duration, regulators, and instruments between money and capital markets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The Indian Financial Market is divided into Money Market and Capital Market. The Money Market caters to short-term needs (less than 1 year), is regulated by RBI, and includes instruments like Treasury Bills, Call Money, Commercial Paper, and Certificate of Deposits. The Capital Market is for long-term wealth creation (greater than 1 year), is regulated by SEBI, and includes Primary (IPO) and Secondary (Stock Market) markets dealing in shares and bonds.
evidenceBullets: Money Market duration is less than 1 year and is regulated by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).
Money Market instruments include Treasury Bills (T-Bills), Call Money, Commercial Paper (CP), and Certificate of Deposits (CDs).
Capital Market duration is greater than 1 year, involves high risk, and is regulated by SEBI.
Capital Market includes the Primary Market for new issues (IPO) and the Secondary Market for trading shares and bonds.

sections.title: Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) and Recent Initiatives
sections.focus: SEBI's statutory status, board composition, and recent frameworks like SWAGAT-FI, REITs, and InvITs.
sections.rationale: SEBI's regulatory powers, board structure, and new frameworks like SWAGAT-FI and REIT/InvIT distinctions are highly probable areas for regulatory body questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: SEBI was established in 1988 and became a statutory body under the SEBI Act of 1992. It has civil court powers for enforcement. Its board includes nominees from the Union Government, Finance Ministry, and RBI. Recently, SEBI introduced the SWAGAT-FI framework for Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) and Foreign Venture Capital Investors (FVCIs). It also reclassified Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) as Equity and relaxed IPO norms. InvITs are similar to REITs but invest in infrastructure.
evidenceBullets: SEBI became a statutory body after the SEBI Act of 1992 and possesses civil court powers under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
The SEBI Board includes a chairman, two Finance Ministry members, one RBI member, and five Union Government nominees.
The SWAGAT-FI framework was introduced for FPIs and Foreign Venture Capital Investors (FVCIs).
REITs allow investment in income-producing real estate, while InvITs invest in infrastructure assets like roads and power lines.

sections.title: Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs)
sections.focus: Definition, regulation, and the three categories of Alternative Investment Funds in India.
sections.rationale: AIF categories (I, II, III) and their specific investment targets (startups vs. debt vs. hedge funds) are standard UPSC economy targets.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs) are privately pooled investment vehicles regulated by SEBI. RBI exempted the government-backed SWAMIH Fund from tightened AIF rules. AIFs are divided into three categories: Category I invests in start-ups, SMEs, and infrastructure; Category II does not use leverage (e.g., Private Equity, Debt, Real Estate Funds); Category III may use leverage and includes Hedge Funds and PIPE.
evidenceBullets: AIFs are privately pooled investment vehicles regulated by SEBI under the 2012 regulations.
Category I AIFs invest in start-ups, early-stage ventures, SMEs, and infrastructure funds.
Category II AIFs do not use leverage and include Private Equity Funds, Debt Funds, and Real Estate Funds.
Category III AIFs may use leverage and include Hedge Funds and Private investment in public equity (PIPE).

sections.title: Equity Derivatives and Front Running
sections.focus: Definitions and mechanisms of equity derivatives and the illegal practice of front running.
sections.rationale: Basic financial market terminology and market manipulation practices are often tested as concept-based questions.
sections.sectionExcerpt: A Derivative is a financial instrument whose value is derived from an underlying asset like equity, metals, or currencies. Futures and Options are common equity derivatives. Front Running is an illegal market manipulation practice where a security is purchased based on advanced non-public information regarding an expected large transaction, undermining market integrity.
evidenceBullets: Derivatives derive their value from underlying assets such as equity shares, commodities, or currencies.
Futures and Options are the most commonly-traded equity derivatives products.
Front Running is an illegal practice of trading based on advanced non-public information about a large pending transaction to manipulate prices.

sections.title: GIFT City IFSC and Foreign Currency Settlement System
sections.focus: Understanding the FCSS, its operator, and the regulatory framework of GIFT City IFSC under IFSCA.
sections.rationale: Distinct topic focusing on India's first IFSC, its regulatory body (IFSCA), and the newly launched FCSS.
sections.sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Finance launched the Foreign Currency Settlement System (FCSS) at GIFT IFSC. It is authorized by IFSCA under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007, to settle foreign currency transactions locally. CCIL IFSC Limited operates it. GIFT City is India's first and only IFSC, established as an SEZ in 2015 and designated as a non-resident zone under FEMA. IFSCA (established 2020) regulates it.
evidenceBullets: FCSS enables local settlement of foreign currency transactions between IFSC Banking Units.
CCIL IFSC Limited is the operator of the FCSS.
GIFT City is India's first and only IFSC, established as an SEZ in 2015.
Designated as a non-resident zone under FEMA.
IFSCA was established in 2020 under the IFSCA Act, 2019 to regulate financial products and institutions in IFSCs.

sections.title: Bond Yield Dynamics and Interest Rates
sections.focus: The inverse relationship between bank interest rates, bond prices, and bond yields.
sections.rationale: A core macroeconomic concept explaining how bond prices and yields react to changes in bank interest rates.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The bond market operates on an equilibrium where bank interest rates inversely affect bond prices. When bank interest rates rise, existing fixed-coupon bond prices fall, causing their effective yield to rise. Conversely, when bank interest rates fall, existing bond prices rise, resulting in a lower yield.
evidenceBullets: When bank interest rates rise, bond prices fall and bond yields rise.
When bank interest rates fall, bond prices rise and bond yields fall.
Higher interest rates make existing bonds with fixed lower coupons less desirable, dropping their price.

sections.title: Specialized Bonds: Transition and Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds
sections.focus: Characteristics and objectives of Transition Bonds and Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds.
sections.rationale: Focuses on specific bond instruments, their regulatory definitions, and their purposes (decarbonization vs. capital adequacy).
sections.sectionExcerpt: Transition Bonds are a sub-category of green debt securities under SEBI, aimed at helping hard-to-abate sectors like steel and cement raise capital for decarbonization. Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds, or perpetual bonds, are issued by banks without a maturity date but include a call option. They are used by banks to meet capital adequacy requirements.
evidenceBullets: Transition Bonds are a sub-category of green debt securities as per SEBI regulations.
Transition Bonds help hard-to-abate sectors raise capital for progressive decarbonization.
Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds are perpetual bonds issued by banks without a maturity date.
AT1 bonds include a call option and are issued to meet capital adequacy requirements.

sections.title: Payments Regulatory Board and Payment Systems Act
sections.focus: Composition, tenure, and functions of the Payments Regulatory Board under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.
sections.rationale: A distinct regulatory and statutory topic detailing the RBI's Payments Regulatory Board structure and the governing 2007 Act.
sections.sectionExcerpt: The RBI constituted the Payments Regulatory Board under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007. The Board consists of the RBI Governor (ex officio Chairperson), Deputy Governor, one RBI officer, and three central government nominees. Members have a 4-year tenure. The Act regulates payment systems in India, with RBI as the central overseeing authority.
evidenceBullets: The Payments Regulatory Board is constituted under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.
The RBI Governor serves as the ex officio Chairperson of the Board.
The Board includes three central government nominated experts with a 4-year tenure.
The Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007 designates RBI as the central authority overseeing payment infrastructure in India.
